food_subgroup: Hot soy-based beverages with traditional sweeteners and herbal additives

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a soy milk entry (FOODON_03311434) but no specific entry for grass jelly soy milk with gula melaka. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers soya milk as a category. No specific code exists for grass jelly or gula melaka variants. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789007 covers soy milk as a substance. No specific concept exists for the grass jelly or gula melaka variant. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one cup), representing a typical single-serve warm beverage portion as sold in Singapore food establishments.

nutrient_source.title: Soy Milk (Unsweetened) and Grass Jelly — component-based estimation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and HPB Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by combining: (1) unsweetened soy milk per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 1097542 (Soymilk, plain, unsweetened), (2) grass jelly (cincau) per HPB Singapore Foodo database, and (3) gula melaka (palm sugar) per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169655 (Sugars, brown). The composite is modelled at approximately 70% soy milk, 15% grass jelly, and 15% gula melaka syrup by weight, reflecting a typical preparation. Values are rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soy milk and palm sugar — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite beverage. GI is estimated as follows: soy milk (unsweetened) has a published GI of approximately 34 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr); gula melaka (palm sugar) has a published GI of approximately 35–54 (Tay et al. 2008, peer-reviewed); grass jelly (cincau) contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrates. A weighted composite GI of approximately 44 is estimated, reflecting the dominant soy milk base with moderate palm sugar contribution. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold. glycemic_load_per_serving = (44 × 10.8 × 300/100) / 100 = 14.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~84 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 84 mg sodium per serving, this beverage is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though the gula melaka content adds calories and should be monitored in the context of overall dietary sodium and sugar intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added palm sugar (gula melaka) contributes free sugars (~25.5 g per 300 g serving) with a moderate glycemic load of 14.3
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this beverage in moderation due to the added gula melaka, which raises the free sugar and glycemic load; requesting reduced sugar or a smaller portion is advisable to limit postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.6 g per 300 g serving), zero cholesterol, and soy isoflavone content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The soy milk base provides heart-beneficial soy isoflavones and polyunsaturated fats with negligible saturated fat and no cholesterol, making this beverage a cardiovascular-friendly choice when consumed without excessive added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Warm Gula Melaka Grass Jelly Soy Milk is a Singaporean warm beverage combining three traditional Southeast Asian ingredients: silky unsweetened soy milk, soft cubes of grass jelly (cincau, made from the Platostoma palustre herb), and gula melaka (Malaccan palm sugar derived from the coconut or arenga palm). The beverage is served warm, distinguishing it from the more common chilled grass jelly drinks found across Singapore and Malaysia. The soy milk provides a creamy, slightly beany base with a mild sweetness, while the grass jelly contributes a subtly bitter, herbal note and a soft, wobbly texture. The gula melaka adds a deep, caramel-like, smoky sweetness with complex molasses undertones that distinguish it from refined white sugar. Nutritionally, the drink provides moderate energy (~186 kcal per 300 g serving), plant-based protein (~7.5 g), low saturated fat, and a low-to-moderate glycemic index of approximately 44, making it a more nutritious alternative to sugar-sweetened beverages. The soy milk base contributes soy isoflavones, which have been associated with cardiovascular benefits. The grass jelly contributes minimal calories and some dietary fibre. The gula melaka contributes free sugars and should be moderated by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those monitoring caloric intake. This beverage is vegan, dairy-free, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. It is commonly found in Singaporean cafes, hawker stalls, and food delivery platforms as a comforting warm drink option.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite beverage. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data using USDA FoodData Central (soy milk, palm sugar) and HPB Singapore Foodo (grass jelly). The GI is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for soy milk and palm sugar from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty, particularly regarding the exact proportion of gula melaka used in the restaurant preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates; no single approved-source entry covers this exact composite beverage. Values should be verified against a laboratory analysis or a direct HPB Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for plant-based soy milk and palm sugar; naturally zero by ingredient composition). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero; all ingredients are plant-derived). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; no animal-derived ingredients present). 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for these ingredients. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the component sources. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for these ingredients. 9. glycemic_index set to 44 as an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold. glycemic_load_per_serving calculated as (44 × 10.8 × 300/100) / 100 = 14.3. 10. The cuisine_tags in the input (Ramen, Pizza, Pasta) appear to be platform-level restaurant tags for Flavours by Saute - Funan and do not reflect the food item itself; these have been disregarded. 11. Serving size of 300 g is estimated based on a standard single-serve warm beverage cup in Singapore food service contexts.
